title: Waiting for Drogo
content:
Agreeable newly-minted officer Giovanni Drogo is posted to Fort Bastiani, a remote outpost standing as the sole guardian of the Northern steppe, home to the legendary Tartars. Initially reluctant to stay, Giovanni’s four month commitment metastasizes into a lifetime as his youthful disregard of Father Time condemns him to underestimate its value. The Tartar Steppe captures the enchanted-like aura of this outpost struggling through the monotony of peacetime. Ironically, it is only upon impending death does Drogo self-actualize by respecting the time he has always taken for granted. 

Fort life is severe in its formality. Soldiers sentenced to tours of duty at The Fort maintain their sanity through illusory dreams of glory from a long-anticipated clash with the enemy that may, in fact, never occur. This pretense of impending conflict serves a dual role; it is both a reprieve from the rigidity and monotony of the Fort, as well as a justification for the maniacal-like adherence to protocol which masks the reality of a wasted life.  While Drogo questions the likelihood of enemy attack, he succumbs to his own delusion—that he has ample time to yet make his mark, to advance, to come into his destiny. The anticipation of a vague but meaningful future enables his resignation to the present’s pointlessness, and his continued isolation engenders complicity rather than skepticism. Regrettably, this collective delusion ultimately traps individuals like Drogo in a cycle of anticipation and stagnation in which they forfeit agency, thus preventing true self-realization and effecting a life devoid of meaningful self-discovery. 

Several years into Drogo’s appointment, when troops notice a distant formation climbing the mountain, the Fort "awakens," recalling the true motives of those within its walls: “it is because of the Tartars that the sentries pace up and down day and night like clockwork”. The sentries' constant vigilance is fueled by hope—some openly, some secretly, and some unconsciously, yet in this pivotal moment their motivations go unexpressed, none “has the courage to speak about it”. When Commander Fillmore is to address the soldiers, he hesitates, unable to confront his dream - “that he had not waited in vain” and that all these years retroactively will become meaningful with this glory. He acquiesces: “Filimore has suddenly seen Fortune approach in silver armor and with a blood-stained sword; he… did not dare to go to meet her”. Borne of years of secret longing, he is unable to confront this perpetual longing when it requires of him most. When he is saved by a letter which tells of the formation as mere scouts from the northern kingdom mapping the land, he downplays his belief in the "so-called Tartar Steppe" and diverts attention to the minutiae of guard formation. A fly buzzing by underscores the meaninglessness of it all. So while this dream of glory is a faux creation seemingly justifying the need for military routines, also serves as "a pretext to give life some significance”. This news telling of the Fort’s inconsequentiality leads to the Fort’s garrison being halved, and Drogo, in a Kafkaesque twist, is forced to stay. Those who remain in the Fort, who were too entrenched in the dream to let go, cling to it even more fiercely. Captain Ortiz, who serves as a king of mentor for Drogo, consoles him by suggesting that hope is merely "some sort of outlet," yet years later, as he leaving the Fort ever the more entranced, he laments to a despondent Drogo that it would be foolish for him to leave, saying, "you still have time...for the war". His persistence in this dream has, like many of the other soldiers allowed him to delude his life away. 

For Drogo, the dream manifests differently. Never fully believing in the dream of false glory, his isolation is justified by the continued delusion that he is forever young and that "the really important things of life are still before him". First journeying to the Fort he mistakes it among the “violet shadows” for another structure and instinctively finds it foreboding, and of “inhospitable and sinister air”. His subconscious recognizes what he himself won't: that Fort life is akin to a tomb. When his initial four month commitment passes, he choses to stay - held back by some enchantment of the Fort and “the onset of his own destiny”. He feels justified in believing he has "much time... before him," yet time swiftly "swallow[s] up the days one after another, one exactly like the other, at a giddy speed”. After this decision, time begins to slip away, with chapters separated by ever-increasing time jumps. This decision is followed by a haunting premonition: Drogo dreams of a procession of phantoms circling outside his window, among them Angustina, a friend who always seems apart, with a "secret pride”, arrogant, and above the trifles others find in the Fort. As the procession floats away, As Angustina has a shared recognition with Drogo - a glance to say he “did not need anyone’s pity” leaving Drogo with an “inhuman nobility”. Like Drogo, Angustina appears apart from the dream of glory, yet unlike Drogo, Angustina possesses an inner soldierly motivation. Years later, as Angustina is sent among a small garrison to match the aforementioned scouts from the north, he fights the blistering cold resisting the pain from his boots ill fit for the task. He refuses to relent, being so proud that he is pushed to the brink of death. As he dies he “drape[s] his cloak” echoing a heroic “painting of Prince Sebastian” and thus somehow dying a heroic death in peacetime. Drogo, jealous he could not join the excursion, reacts with more jealousy, seemingly because Angustina has escaped the Fort and achieved glory despite being apart - that he has some inherent means. Drogo's false notion of life as always before him is just a defense against his failure to have any self-motivated reason to stay. Drogo sees Angustina's death as an escape but also as a vindication he was robbed of, yet one which requires the self-fulfillment Drogo lacks. In discussing it with Ortiz, he is told he could have joined: "you had only to ask". Drogo's failures continue. 

Returning to the city to request a transfer from the Fort, he fails to assert himself to the Colonel and is forced to stay, learning of the Fort's downsizing and that others have already submitted their requests. There, he meets Maria, an old friend, but fails to confess his love, perpetually reminded of the Fort: "the strip of sunlight [reminding him] of the Fort". Lost in the labyrinth of the Fort, Drogo is forever wrapped in notions of what life could be, and so fails to act. 

Returning from his month's leave after 20 days, after failing to relate to Maria and even his mother, he "does not feel that he has changed... Drogo perseveres in his illusion that the really important things of life are still before him”. Thus, Drogo squanders his last years, oblivious to its passing. It is only until he sees a reflection of himself in the newly-minted officer Moro, who arrives as he did when he first met Ortiz, that he realizes "an entire generation had been used up, that he had now passed the peak of life". Drogo passes his final years, deteriorating as the northerners finally come to attack. Now second in command, Drogo is bedridden and only learns of their arrival through a chance encounter with the fort tailor. Simeoni, now commandant of the Fort and a former friend who once shared eager conspiracies of a road being built in the Steppe with Drogo, tells him he needs to retire so that reinforcements can use his room. Drogo, protesting, invokes the perennial dream of the Fort. He struggles to express: “you know what I mean," but Simeoni rejects him. As life passes Drogo once more, he notices a streak of sunlight, once more perhaps symbolizing this very passage brought on by the delusions of the Fort. Pushed out of the Fort, and despite realizing that he is truly alone, Drogo finally is able to let go of his false dream. As he dies he, “dare[s] to imagine that everything was not finished, because perhaps his great moment had come, the decisive battle which might make his whole life worthwhile”. Further the passage uses “violet shadows” circling back to when Drogo first visited the fort - he has finally come full circle and has gone beyond the its darkness and delusions. Finally, he ceases to envy Angustina and, for the first time, makes a heroic choice of himself. Letting go of the myth, in his final hours with life clearly at its end, he is finally able to make a choice for himself and create his own meaning and understanding of the self. 

Buzzati masterfully crafts a haunting narrative portraying the existential plight of individuals caught in waiting. The Fort which serves as a reminder of stagnation and unfulfilled dreams, envelopes the soldiers’ consciousnesses. Drogo once a naive dreamer, who passes life on the sidelines - later being tormented by that which passed him, encapsulates the struggle for self-meaning in a world in which individuals can seem truly alone. It warns against holding illusions and being stuck in anticipation - “Is it far yet?… it is better further on and we move off again unhurriedly” and then only too late “turn[ing] round, almost instinctively, and see[ing] that a gate has been bolted behind us”. Drogo’s resignation in enchantment in the Fort mirrors our struggle to find meaning in the mundane and thus possible passivity in confronting life. Ultimately it cautions against holding hopes which trap us in cycles of aforementioned anticipation and stagnation, only to later look back and see all that has passed.
excerpt: On the illusion of time and weight of the unlived.
